subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Raw and processed data tables include clear headings, units, trial labels and uncertainty columns, enhancing clarity and precision.
Graphs are well-labelled with error bars, and uncertainty propagation for key quantities shows quantitative engagement.
Appropriate consideration of uncertainties in angle, length and mass measurements is evident.
weaknesses: Data processing splits the dataset at an arbitrary 0.4 J breakpoint without statistical or physical justification.
Uncertainty propagation uses simple summation rather than root-sum-square, leading to potential overestimation.
Regression analysis lacks statistical justification (no standard error on slopes) and inconsistencies appear in fit selection across regimes.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The candidate communicated data recording and processing clearly and considered uncertainties thoroughly (bands 5â6 and 5â6), but data processing quality falls into the 3â4 band due to conceptual and statistical omissions. An overall score of 4 balances strong presentation with notable analytical weaknesses.

strengths: The research question is precisely stated within a focused, appropriate physical context, embedding friction and rubber-band hysteresis on an air-track.
A labelled diagram, step-by-step procedure, apparatus list with resolutions and uncertainty specifications support reproducibility with minimal ambiguity.
A comprehensive control-variable table explains why each factor matters, how it is held constant, and justifies multiple trials, reflecting thoughtful methodological planning.
weaknesses: Real-world relevance (e.g., applications in launch-and-return mechanisms) is only briefly mentioned, missing an opportunity to deepen context.
Quantitative characterization of the rubber-band stiffness (k) is omitted, reducing reproducibility for future investigators.
Minor procedural details such as rubber-band material and pretension method are not fully specified.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three Research Design strands were placed firmly in the 5â6 band, showing a specific context, reproducible methodology, and explained considerations. Slight omissions in broader relevance and quantitative characterization prevent a full 6, so a score of 5 accurately reflects the high quality with room for minor improvement.

strengths: The conclusion is consistent with the processed data and justifies the observed trends in energy loss, linking them to friction and hysteresis.
Acknowledges large uncertainties and qualitatively relates findings to physical phenomena like rubber-band saturation.
weaknesses: Asserts a polynomial trend without fitting higher-order models or providing residual analysis to support nonlinearity.
Limited quantitative comparison to published literature values (e.g., drag coefficients, hysteresis curves), leading to a somewhat superficial scientific context.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is well-supported by data (band 5â6) but lacks full consistency in quantitative backing (band 3â4), earning a middle-band score of 4 for being justified yet incomplete in scientific comparison and statistical validation.

strengths: Specific methodological weaknesses (e.g., compound uncertainties, track displacement) are identified and linked to their relative impact on results.
Realistic and relevant improvements (rigidly fixing the track, using video analysis, widening GPE range) are described with explanations of how they address cited weaknesses.
weaknesses: The relative impact of each identified weakness is not quantified, limiting assessment depth.
Suggestions (e.g., measuring track movement) are made but lack detailed implementation plans or target metrics.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation describes specific limitations and proposes realistic, relevant improvements, placing both strands in the 3â4 band. Without quantitative assessment of impacts or fully detailed implementation, a score of 4 appropriately reflects solid but not exhaustive evaluative depth.

strandSelections.reasoning: The research question is explicitly stated, includes the physical quantities, apparatus, and the numerical angle used, and is embedded in a focused discussion of friction and rubber-band hysteresis on an air-track.  The introduction links these ideas to conservation of energy and clearly explains why the question is worth asking in this context.  This specificity satisfies the descriptor for a âspecific and appropriate contextâ, although a brief mention of real-world relevance (e.g., launch-and-return mechanisms) could have strengthened it further, so a mid-band (5) rather than the full 6 is appropriate.

Percentage and absolute uncertainties are propagated for distance, height, GPE, and energy loss; error bars appear on all graphs; uncertainty sources (angle, length, mass) are considered quantitatively.  While the propagation sometimes adds instead of quadratically combining uncertainties, the treatment remains broadly appropriate and complete, justifying a band 5 score.

A substantial control-variable table explains why each factor matters, how it is held constant, and gives values with uncertainties.  The student also justifies five trials per height, treats rubber-band hysteresis as a limiting factor, and later discusses systematic displacement of the track.  These amount to an explanation (not merely description) of considerations for collecting relevant and sufficient data, meeting the 5-6 band; slight repetition and a few missing quantitative justifications warrant 5 rather than 6.

Specific weaknesses are describedâcompound uncertainties, air-track displacement, large error barsâand the student comments on their influence (e.g., track tilt lowering rebound height).  The relative impact is touched upon but not quantitatively evaluated, hence band 3-4 with the upper score of 4.

The student supplies a labelled diagram, a numbered step-by-step procedure, apparatus list with measurement resolutions, and explains how Logger Pro is used to extract distances.  Distances, angles, and timings are all specified with uncertainties, meaning another investigator could reproduce the work with little ambiguity.  Minor omissions (e.g., air-track length tolerance, precise rubber-band stiffness) prevent a perfect 6, but the description is comfortably within the top band.

Averages, uncertainty propagation, linear fits and correlation coefficients are provided, demonstrating solid processing.  However, separating the data set into two arbitrary regions (<0.4 J and >0.4 J) without statistical justification, together with an inconsistent treatment of polynomial expectations vs. linear fitting, introduces conceptual weaknesses.  Thus the work falls into the 3-4 band with a high score of 4.

Proposed improvements (rigidly fixing track, using video analysis, widening GPE range, reducing drag) are realistic and linked to the cited weaknesses; the explanations show how each change would reduce specific errors or extend validity.  Depth is solid but not exhaustive, so a 3-4 band with score 4 is appropriate.

Raw and processed tables include headings, units, trial labels and uncertainty columns.  Graphs have axis labels and error bars.  Some captions are verbose and a few labels overlap, but overall the presentation is both clear (readable, logically ordered) and precise (units, significant figures consistent), meriting band 5-6; a score of 5 reflects the minor formatting glitches.

The student explains frictional heating, terminal velocity and hysteresis qualitatively and cites sources, but there is limited quantitative comparison to published coefficients or literature curves.  The discussion remains relevant yet somewhat superficial, matching the middle band (3-4) at a score of 3.

The conclusion restates the observed non-linear increase in energy loss and links it to friction and rubber-band saturation, using gradients extracted from graphs.  Although generally consistent with processed data, the claim of a âpolynomialâ trend is not quantitatively demonstrated and the large uncertainties acknowledged elsewhere are not fully factored into the strength of the claim, so band 3-4 with score 4 is most fitting.

annotations.comment: The background equation uses g=9.8 mÂ·sâ»Â², whereas later calculations use 9.76 mÂ·sâ»Â². Ensure consistent use of gravitational acceleration throughout to avoid systematic bias.

The method describes measuring height via trigonometry in detail, but adding a direct measurement (e.g., laser or video analysis) could reduce cumulative trigonometric uncertainties and streamline data collection.
The controlled-variable table is comprehensive, but it omits quantitative characterization of rubber-band stiffness (k). Including an experimental measurement of k would improve reproducibility.

The research question is precisely stated within a focused discussion of friction and rubber-band hysteresis on an angled air-track, embedding it in a clear physical context. This specificity supports reproducibility and demonstrates strong design choices.
Some percentage uncertainties exceed 20%, particularly in mid-range GPE points, indicating low precision. Explore methods such as repeated trials or improved sensor alignment to reduce error bars.
The addition of eye-fitted minimum and maximum gradients lacks statistical justification. Consider calculating uncertainties in slope using standard error from regression.
Graph #1 is split at 0.4 J without justification; the choice of breakpoint should be supported by a physical or statistical criterion.

The scatter suggests a linear trend, but the narrative also describes the computer-generated best-fit as âundetectable.â Clarify whether fitting was constrained by software settings or data characteristics.

Graph #4 applies a single linear fit across two distinct regimes below and above 0.4 J, contradicting earlier segmentation. Reconcile the analysis by applying separate fits or justifying a unified trend.
The stepwise procedure and diagram allow reproducibility, but step 2 could specify the rubber-band material and pretension method to avoid variations in hysteresis behaviour.
Raw and processed data tables include clear headings, units and uncertainties, enhancing both clarity and precision of data recording.
Uncertainty propagation is handled by simple summation of percentage errors rather than quadrature, leading to potential overestimation. Consider using root-sum-square for independent uncertainties.
Interpreting the gradient as an efficiency metric requires linking m to energy conversion factors. Provide a clear physical rationale connecting slope to frictional or hysteresis losses.
Graph #3âs minimum gradient is negative, which is physically impossible for efficiency. This highlights that error bars are too large to support reliable fitting in this regime.

Displacement of the air-track by the collision is identified as a weakness, but its impact on measured bounce height is not quantified. Consider measuring track movement to estimate induced GPE error.
Proposal to use video analysis to reduce trigonometric steps is realistic and could significantly decrease propagation of small measurement errors.
The conclusion asserts a polynomial trend without fitting higher-order models or providing statistical comparison. Add a quadratic fit or residual analysis to justify the nonlinearity claim.

The discussion refers to terminal velocity qualitatively but lacks quantitative comparison (e.g., drag coefficient or literature hysteresis curves). Integrate published values to strengthen scientific context.
The stated limitations include calculation complexity but do not assess the relative impact of each source. Quantify how much the trigonometric step contributes to total uncertainty.
Attaching the air-track firmly to the lab-jack is a relevant improvement; describe precisely how this will be implemented and tested to ensure consistent angle maintenance.
Expanding the GPE range by adjusting the angle and reducing drag is actionable and addresses data sparsity; consider specifying target values to guide further trials.
Several bibliography entries lack consistent formatting (e.g., missing italics or inconsistent access date styles). Adopt a uniform citation style (e.g., APA or MLA) for professionalism.

rubricData.rubricSpecificInfo: âThe Physics Internal Assessment (IA) is a critical component of the IB Diploma Programme, providing students with the opportunity to design and conduct independent investigations into physics phenomena. This assessment emphasizes the development of practical skills, analytical thinking, and a deep understanding of scientific methodologies. By engaging in the IA, students not only apply theoretical knowledge to real-world scenarios but also cultivate competencies in research design, data analysis, and critical evaluation, all of which are essential for academic and professional success in scientific fields. â
rubricData.name: Physics IA
